So, praise is important to activate liking, but how and when you praise people and give out compliments can matter a lot. I explain how to do so in the following video. There are two especially effective ways to amplify the impact of your compliments. First, give a compliment behind a deserving person's back. Praising people behind their backs can be more effective than telling them directly. Suppose you're at work and in a meeting your boss says something you consider very smart. You can't just say so right then, excellent idea there boss, as that would appear self-serving. So what could you do instead? During a coffee break or at the end of the meeting, tell the boss's assistant of your view. You know, I thought what Sandy said about our next step forward was brilliant. Several outcomes are likely. One is that because people want to be associated with good news, the assistant will no doubt tell your boss what you said. Because you didn't offer your assessment for the boss's ears, no one should assign you an unattractive ulterior motive. And then finally, your boss will be more likely to believe your praise and like you more for it. Now, the second particularly effective form of praise is to compliment people when they've done a good thing you'd like them to continue doing. That way they'd be motivated to do more of the good thing in the future in order to live up to the admirable reputation you've given them. What's the implication? If there's someone who ordinarily performs commendably, perhaps a conscientious colleague who often comes prepared for meetings or a helpful friend who frequently tries hard to give useful input on your ideas, compliment them not just on the behavior, but instead on the trait they've exhibited. You'll probably see more of it. Research supports this conclusion. People who have been praised for their conscientiousness on a task perform more conscientiously on a related task days afterward. Similar, adults complimented on their helpful tendencies became significantly more helpful in a separate setting much later. Now, of course, in all cases, the praise should be honest.
